title: Electron Configuration and Orbital Notation
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Write electron configurations using the Aufbau principle, Hund's rule, and the Pauli exclusion principle
Draw orbital diagrams showing electron spin in each orbital
Write configurations for ions and identify exceptions for chromium and copper

sections.heading: The Three Rules of Electron Filling
sections.content:
Electrons fill orbitals according to three rules:

1. Aufbau Principle (building up): Electrons fill the lowest-energy orbitals first. The filling order is: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d, 5p, 6s, 4f, 5d, 6p... The diagonal rule (drawing arrows from top-right to bottom-left on an energy level chart) helps you remember this order.

2. Pauli Exclusion Principle: Each orbital holds a maximum of two electrons, and they must have opposite spins (one spin-up ↑, one spin-down ↓). This is why the s subshell (1 orbital) holds 2 electrons, p (3 orbitals) holds 6, d (5 orbitals) holds 10, and f (7 orbitals) holds 14.

3. Hund's Rule: When filling orbitals of equal energy (like the three 2p orbitals), electrons spread out — one per orbital with parallel spins first — before pairing up. Think of it like passengers on a bus: everyone takes their own seat before sitting next to a stranger.

sections.heading: Writing Configurations: Oxygen and Iron
sections.content:
Oxygen (Z = 8): 1s² 2s² 2p⁴
  Orbital diagram:
  1s: ↑↓   2s: ↑↓   2p: ↑↓ ↑ ↑
  (The 2p has 4 electrons: by Hund's rule, fill each orbital singly first, then pair)

Iron (Z = 26): 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶
  Noble gas shorthand: [Ar] 4s² 3d⁶
  ([Ar] represents 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ — the first 18 electrons)

  3d orbital diagram: ↑↓ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑
  (6 electrons in 5 d-orbitals: one pair and four singles)

For ions, remove electrons from the highest principal energy level first:
  Fe²⁺: [Ar] 3d⁶ (remove the two 4s electrons first, NOT 3d)
  Fe³⁺: [Ar] 3d⁵ (remove 4s² then one 3d)

sections.heading: Why Configuration Matters
sections.content:
An element's electron configuration determines its chemical behavior. The outermost electrons — called valence electrons — are the ones involved in bonding. Elements in the same group of the periodic table have the same valence electron configuration, which is why they have similar chemical properties.

Group 1 (alkali metals): [noble gas] ns¹ — one valence electron, easily lost → +1 ions
Group 17 (halogens): [noble gas] ns² np⁵ — seven valence electrons, readily gain one → −1 ions
Group 18 (noble gases): [noble gas] ns² np⁶ — full valence shell → extremely stable, rarely react

The entire structure of the periodic table — its groups, periods, blocks (s, p, d, f) — is a direct map of electron configuration patterns.

Electron configurations follow three rules: Aufbau (lowest energy first), Pauli (max 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins), and Hund's (spread out before pairing). For transition metal ions, remove electrons from the s orbital before the d orbital.

sections.heading: The Chromium and Copper Exceptions
sections.content:
You would predict chromium (Z = 24) to be [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴, but its actual configuration is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵. Similarly, copper (Z = 29) is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d¹⁰ instead of the expected [Ar] 4s² 3d⁹.

Why? Half-filled and fully-filled d subshells are especially stable due to the symmetrical distribution of electron density and favorable exchange energy (a quantum mechanical effect). The energy gained by having 3d⁵ (half-filled) or 3d¹⁰ (fully-filled) outweighs the energy cost of moving one electron from 4s to 3d.

These exceptions reinforce a deeper point: the Aufbau filling order is a useful approximation, but real atoms are governed by quantum mechanics, and sometimes a configuration with lower total energy does not follow the simple filling rules. On the AP exam, you are expected to know these two exceptions and explain why they occur.

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
